speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Sunday, November 10, 1996. Interview with Eva Eiseman in Highland Park, Illinois. The interview will be conducted in English. My name is Peggy Marc Kaz. My name is Peggy Marc Kaz. I'm conducting an interview with Eva Eiseman. Today is Sunday, November 10, 1996. Tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Eiseman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva-- Eva Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Hamburg, Germany, April 14, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are how old today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start with some of your family background. How long had your family been in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family was in Germany for four generations. Matter of fact, in Hamburg, Germany for four generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in Nienburg, in the southern part of Germany. And her family-- her mother came from Posen, which was on the border of Germany and Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your father's family come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally they came from Holland, from the Netherlands. But I don't know very much about that part of his life, because he was born in Hamburg, and so was his father born in Hamburg already. But my grandfather's family came from Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do? What was their profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents' profession? They had small furniture stores all over Hamburg. And they really started that. And they were in business for probably about 25, 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father go into the family business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was with a large import export firm in Hamburg, [? Simon ?] Israel and Company was the name of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they deal with?

speaker: interviewee
text: They dealt with-- you know what, I don't know exactly. I really don't. I think it was probably clothing, men's clothing. And I'm not very sure what else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your home, the house you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born into a beautiful world. There was a private clinic across from where my parents lived, and that's where I was born. We had a beautiful apartment with a sun room and with a music room. And I had both sets of grandparents. One set of grandparents lived in Hamburg. And the other set of grandparents lived in Nienburg, where we visited very often. And when I was two and a half years old, my little brother was born. And we grew up very happily and very nicely. I have really-- life was, in those days, really very even. I think that the inflation was one part that was difficult at the-- but I can't remember that, because I was really too little. As I grew up, we would often visit my grandparents in Nienburg. And my parents started to travel when I was very little. And we had a wonderful time in Nienburg, because there were two maids. One of them was always sewing my dresses. So when I came back home, I always had a whole little wardrobe of new dresses. And we played-- I learned how to swim there. And we played-- it was a small town. My grandparents had also a store. But it was men's clothing-- shoes and men's clothing-- and he became very well-to-do. He was very, very religious. Synagogue was pretty much across the street. And we visited very often. It was a big part of my growing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was on your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on my mother's side, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Sally Hildesheimer. And my grandmother's name was Johanna Hildesheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on your father's side, what were those grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: John-- John Jacob Cohen. And my grandmother's name was Dora Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tia Hildesheimer Cohen, and Siegbert Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit more about your childhood. What kind of a neighborhood did you grow up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We grew up in a beautiful neighborhood, with mostly large apartment houses and parks. I have pictures when-- when I was little, my parents took me to the park. And my school was very close by as well. Just a few blocks up the street. And it was a private Jewish school. We learned Hebrew right along with history and arithmetic. So while I can't speak Hebrew, I can still, you know, read it fluently. And oh, those were wonderful years. I was pretty popular in school. And when-- we had many birthday parties. And my grandmother took me shopping downtown Hamburg. And we went often on trips. We went to the seashore, went to the mountainside [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my parents. And I really only have very, very good, fond memories. And as I said before, when, when there were vacation times, we went to visit the grandparents in Nienburg. And my parents started traveling, as I-- as I said before, quite extensively. And when they did, they often went with my grandparents, my father's parents. And then we would go to visit my mother's grandparents-- my mother's parents. And so they took care of us. My mother always said the air was so much better where they lived. But I think she just wanted to get rid of us and have a good time traveling herself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have really-- we were good friends with my teachers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents took me to the theater, and my parents took me to the museums. And it just was a very happy childhood. Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were really mostly the children from school. And they were mostly Jewish children. Although, in the neighborhood where I lived, there were many non-Jewish children that I played with as well. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] children from all around, really. When I had my birthday parties, though, I think I invited mostly the children from my class, I remember distinctly. And I have the picture that maybe later on we can show from my eighth year birthday party.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now going back to your earliest memories of childhood, did you sense from your non-Jewish playmates any difference between you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. No, not at all, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or from the neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents themselves had many non-Jewish friends as well. No, not at all. Mm mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's talk a little bit more about school. You said it was a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was to Jewish school. We, We had-- you know, we learned-- rather than English, I learned French, first, as a second language. And it was very small classes. Maybe 20, 22 children. And Hebrew, as I said, Hebrew was a big part of learning. Arithmetic, and we had, you know, it was a well-rounded school. It was like from there we went on to the realschule, which was four years of grade school. And then came realschule, which was [SPEAKING GERMAN]. But that, of course, was interrupted because we had to leave Germany. But I must say that in all honesty, I find that the eight years that I went to school in Germany gave me a very sound base of education. I always feel that I built whatever I did later on in life upon that education that I received there. It was excellent, excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Little girls school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your religious background.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and mother were very deeply religious people. We had always Friday night in our house, always guests for Friday night in our house. My mother would light the candles. My father would make kiddish. Friday night was absolutely nothing but dinner at home with all the trimmings. And very often-- my father's parents lived up the street. Very often Friday night after dinner, we would go up the street to my grandparents' house, where my father's brother and his wife would come, and other friends and relatives. So Friday night was always wonderful. When I was about maybe eight or nine, my grandparents from Nienburg-- my father-- my grandfather retired and moved to Hamburg. And he also was a very religious person. And we had wonderful seders in my grandfather's house. The first part was always my grandfather's part. And then the second part of the Seder that was mostly singing, my father was the one who took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we always tell such a funny story. [LAUGHS] On the second day of Seder as you know, the Seder plate comes back on the table. But the glass, the wine glass, was empty. So my grandmother asked the maid, what happened to the wine in the wine glass? And she said, the men didn't come, so I drank it. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] didn't come, so she drank it. So we always had wonderful times together. And I still, later on, like to show you, I still have the kippah that my grandfather wore when he led the Seder. So my father also was very friendly with the rabbi of our synagogue. It was called [GERMAN]. It a large, conservative temple. And it was Dr. Carlebach, a very famous German rabbi, who became a good friend of my father's. We would go for some [INAUDIBLE], we would go for all the festivities.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always had wonderful Hanukkahs. I remember that my grandmother and her maid brought up a laundry basket with presents for Hanukkah. So we were just loaded. And it was very interesting because right after, of course, came Christmas, Weihnachten. And it was interesting because, I told you, my parents had many friends that were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Christmas Eve, she would take us to their houses. And we would have big plates of lebkuchen and all the goodies, marzipan, that we would get from my parents' friends. We would go downtown and see the windows and the Christmas trees.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came back home, this is how it was explained to me when I was just a little girl. Christmas is a beautiful holiday for you to look at. But it doesn't belong to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So therefore, we were never-- we never had any questions or qualms, or were envious, none of that. Because we understood that it was a beautiful holiday we could look at, and we could partake if we wanted to. But it didn't belong to us. So it was a very clear message that helped me, actually, to bring my own children up that way. You mentioned that the synagogue that you belonged to was conservative.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the women-- actually, I guess here in the United States, you would call it orthodox. Because men and women did not sit together. Men and women-- the women were sitting upstairs, and the men were sitting downstairs. And you-- each one had their designated seat where you were sitting, so you knew. And when it was [INAUDIBLE], we could run down as children. We could collect all the candies that we had. But we always had a kosher household. And as I said, my parents were very, very traditional religious. Not orthodox, but very traditional and religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that did not interfere with their relations with their non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends for holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house? Really not, no. That I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to when you were eight years old. You would have been in third grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was 1933, the year that Hitler rose to power. Tell me what you remember of the changes that started to take place in '33. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the very first change that I noticed-- that my parents moved away from that beautiful apartment that we had when I was born, to a neighborhood that was more strictly Jewish and who had a temple right there. And then the next thing was that we couldn't have a nanny or a maid anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could only have a Jewish maid, and that was almost nonexistent. And that's really how it started. And then, oftentimes, when we would come home from school, little by little, it would start that they would call Jude after us. And, well, and then, of course, when Hitler, after six months or so that he was in power, they were starting to march. And we got more familiar with the SS and the SA, as well as they had a newspaper called which was a Nazi newspaper that made caricatures out of Jewish people, with very ugly names, and so on and so forth. But our life went on. We went to school, our life went on. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] business really was not any worse. I mean, my father didn't notice business-wise. We lived, as I said, in Hamburg. And Hamburg was a very international, large city. And I think people in smaller towns in the southern part of Germany probably noticed it quicker. But my parents, as I said, for four generations lived in Hamburg. My grandfather's second cousin was Max Warburg, the very famous banker. And so that-- we just sort of really pushed it away, and didn't pay much attention to it. Because in reality, they just never thought, no matter what Hitler would do, it would pass. It wouldn't stay that way. And, and if it didn't pass, it wouldn't really attack them, not them. They were who they were, and they just felt they would not be touched. And so time went on. And it got-- the news was worse. You know, you would hear on the news bad things. And I remember some people started leaving to the United States, or other parts. But my parents didn't budge at all. And I remember as late as 1938, maybe the spring of 1938, when the movement was much, much stronger by that time. We were at '33, so that was five years later. And Max Warburg, this famous banker-- he was really in Germany what the Rothschilds were in France-- did a fact-finding tour all through Europe, and came back and had a dinner for the family. And said, you know what, as bad as it is here, it's much worse in the other countries. And almost-- not almost-- did say, don't leave. And my grandfather Cohen, my father's father, said there you are. And you want to know it better than Max Warburg. They simply did not believe it would hit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about the situation with your friends or with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes we did, as we grew older. When we were nine and ten, matter of fact, two of the friends that I was lucky enough to meet later on in life again, I remember I was playing with one of them, Lilo Baer was her name. Her family had shoe stores all over Germany. And we were playing, and the mail came. And they already had hoped that they could immigrate to the United States. And she was so anxious that we took the letter and over steam-- we had a kettle, and we steamed the letter open. And she could see that they would get the affidavit from some relatives that they had in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, we did discuss it. We did discuss it. We got scared now. As two or three years passed, we became frightened. And we wondered what would happen to us, we really did. What things took place that frightened you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for one thing, beginning of 1938, the Gestapo started to come to pick up the men. And so they came to my house numerous times. And my father already would be visiting friends and customers and relatives all over Germany so that they wouldn't find him at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew not to be home?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew not to be home at that point, right. He knew not to be home. And ultimately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they almost caught him. So it was a very scary, scary thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can tell you the story how that happened. I was alone in my apartment. I was writing letters to a friend of mine who had already immigrated to the United States. And my grandparents lived downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Gestapo came to my house, they said-- they gave me a push. And they said [SPEAKING GERMAN]? And no, I said, I can swear that he isn't home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. If Jews give their word, that's no word at all. And they gave me a push and I fell. And they ransacked the house. And while they were doing that, I went down the servants' entrance to, to go downstairs to my grandparents where my father was, and whispered, they're upstairs. And with that-- the car of the Gestapo was outside. So with that, my grandmother was like-- very late at night-- my grandmother took my father, and they left through the servants' door, walked by the car of the Gestapo, and went to a doctor that was a Catholic doctor, who gave my father some kind of a certificate. He-- my father was-- First World War, '14-'18, he was in the war. And he had the Iron Cross, and he had things that were wrong still from the war. But he really hadn't suffered from those at this point anymore. But this nice doctor gave him a certificate and told him-- got him into a Catholic hospital. And that's where he was for a couple of weeks, until my mother could figure out what we were going to do, in order to get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother at the time that this took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was, I think, downstairs as well, with my father and my grandparents. But she didn't go with-- she stayed with my grandfather. Just my grandmother and my father went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they were after your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was a Jew. They were after every Jew. That's-- they were after every Jew. And that's why if you didn't leave by that time, then all hell broke loose. And everybody was picked up regardless. And then the whole disaster started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to your parents? Or did they talk to you about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. Our house was always very serene, and very cultured, and very calm. And we weren't burdened with that. We were not burdened with that. Until there was no more secret, the story that I just told. So then, then all hell broke loose. And then we had to see how we got out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before all hell broke loose, did you at any time think about leaving, or escaping or-- did it occur to you that this was something that you may have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it occurred to us it's something we would have to do. We wanted to come to the United States, but it was impossible. I still have the little slip where the quota was so high, that if we had waited for our a number, well, I wouldn't be here to tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother during all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to school as well. He went to the [NON-ENGLISH] school, just where, where my father went. And it's also a Jewish school. And he had his own friends. And my parents took us on trips, you know, to the mountains oftentimes. And we were close. My brother and I were close. I mean not as-- if I had had a little sister, I would have been probably closer. But as brother and sisters go, we had a-- we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the two of you talk about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really not. Only after it blew up in our faces, then. Really not so much before. I am sure my parents with each other, and my parents with my grandparents. we were really not burdened.

speaker: interviewer
text: They tried to protect you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They did. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how the events affected your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, little by little, customers would take the business away. But very little by little. It-- I think maybe up to '36 or '37, his business was still OK. And it just started, I would say, in '38 that really-- now we didn't have any help in the house. Now they're called us Jews. And now, business did get lack-- you know, get-- got much more lax. And in 1938, it was very, very noticeable. And we knew then that something had to be done. But it was always that we would like to come to the United States, and that was just not possible for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the very end, my grandmother knew the secretary of the Uruguayan consul in Hamburg. And she was very wealthy, so with giving her money and doing what she could, she ultimately got a tourist visa for my parents and us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, the big problem was a tourist visa-- just what it says. You can go for a year out to that country, but you have to come back. And then that was the most horrendous story that I have to tell up to that point. Because the only way that you would really receive that tourist visa was if the head of the Gestapo in Hamburg gave you permission to return to Germany, to return to Hamburg. Well, you know that that was-- obviously, nobody would do that. And I remember, my brother and I were outside-- that was during the time that my father was in the Catholic hospital. And my mother went in to the office of the head of the Gestapo in Hamburg. And I don't know how long she was in there. 15 minutes, 20 minutes, half an hour, I don't know. It seemed a long time to us. We were waiting outside, my brother and I. And my mother came out of that office drenched, just tears, and drenched. And she had this little piece of paper, kind of rumpled in her hand. And she said, come on, let's go. I got the permit. And with that, we then went to the hospital, and we had to get my father discharged and packed our suitcases-- still ran downtown to get a couple of more new dresses. But in reality, just with one suitcase-- my parents, as I told you at the beginning of this interview, were travellers. And they were very-- on very good terms with the management of Hamburg Sud, which is still a large company, bought ships. And somehow or other, we did get on the [GERMAN], 11, 12 o'clock at night. And that was the story that I'll just never forget, with my four grandparents sitting there-- standing there, not sitting there. Standing there at the harbor in Hamburg. And that was horrible, horrible, horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break now. And then we'll talk about that story during the second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, fine.
